foreshadow_text: True!—nervous—very, very dreadfully nervous I had been and am; but why will you say that I am mad? The disease had sharpened my senses—not destroyed—not dulled them. Above all was the sense of hearing acute. I heard all things in the heaven and in the earth. I heard many things in hell. How, then, am I mad? Hearken! and observe how healthily—how calmly I can tell you the whole story.
payoff_text:
No doubt I now grew _very_ pale;—but I talked more fluently, and with a heightened voice. Yet the sound increased—and what could I do? It was a low, dull, quick sound—much such a sound as a watch makes when enveloped in cotton. I gasped for breath—and yet the officers heard it not. I talked more quickly—more vehemently; but the noise steadily increased. I arose and argued about trifles, in a high key and with violent gesticulations; but the noise steadily increased. Why would they not be gone? I paced the floor to and fro with heavy strides, as if excited to fury by the observations of the men—but the noise steadily increased. Oh God! what could I do? I foamed—I raved—I swore! I swung the chair upon which I had been sitting, and grated it upon the boards, but the noise arose over all and continually increased. It grew louder—louder—louder! And still the men chatted pleasantly, and smiled. Was it possible they heard not? Almighty God!—no, no! They heard!—they suspected!—they knew!—they were making a mockery of my horror!—this I thought, and this I think. But anything was better than this agony! Anything was more tolerable than this derision! I could bear those hypocritical smiles no longer! I felt that I must scream or die! and now—again!—hark! louder! louder! louder! _louder!_

“Villains!” I shrieked, “dissemble no more! I admit the deed!—tear up the planks!—here, here!—It is the beating of his hideous heart!”
foreshadow_summary: The narrator claims his disease has sharpened his senses, especially his hearing, allowing him to hear all things in heaven, earth, and hell.
payoff_summary: The narrator hears a heartbeat-like sound that grows louder, believes the police officers also hear it, and finally confesses to the murder, screaming about the beating of the old man's heart.

proposed_trigger.description: The police officers accept the narrator's invitation to sit and rest in the old man's chamber, directly above the concealed corpse. The narrator begins to feel unwell, hears a ringing in his ears, which becomes more distinct and transforms into the sound of a beating heart.
observable_conditions: Police officers enter and sit in the chamber (P0015)
Narrator feels pale, has a headache, and imagines a ringing in his ears (P0016)
The ringing becomes more distinct and is recognized as the low, dull, quick sound of a heartbeat (P0017)

relation_description: The narrator's early claim of acute hearing is the foundation for his later auditory hallucination of the heartbeat, which directly leads to his confession.
stage1_rationale: The acute hearing is prominently introduced in the opening paragraph and explicitly paid off in the climax when the narrator hears the imagined heartbeat, forming a clear delayed setup and payoff with non-trivial narrative distance.

foreshadow_text: And have I not told you that what you mistake for madness is but over-acuteness of the sense?—now, I say, there came to my ears a low, dull, quick sound, such as a watch makes when enveloped in cotton. I knew that sound well, too. It was the beating of the old man’s heart. It increased my fury, as the beating of a drum stimulates the soldier into courage.
payoff_text: No doubt I now grew _very_ pale;—but I talked more fluently, and with a heightened voice. Yet the sound increased—and what could I do? It was a low, dull, quick sound—much such a sound as a watch makes when enveloped in cotton. I gasped for breath—and yet the officers heard it not. I talked more quickly—more vehemently; but the noise steadily increased. I arose and argued about trifles, in a high key and with violent gesticulations; but the noise steadily increased. I foamed—I raved—I swore! I swung the chair upon which I had been sitting, and grated it upon the boards, but the noise arose over all and continually increased. It grew louder—louder—louder! They heard!—they suspected!—they knew!—they were making a mockery of my horror!—this I thought, and this I think. But anything was better than this agony! foreshadow_summary: When the narrator first hears the old man's heartbeat before the murder, he describes it as 'a low, dull, quick sound, such as a watch makes when enveloped in cotton.'
payoff_summary: During the police visit, the narrator hears the same sound again, described with the exact same phrase: 'a low, dull, quick sound—much such a sound as a watch makes when enveloped in cotton.'

proposed_trigger.description: The police officers' prolonged presence in the chamber, combined with the narrator's growing anxiety and guilt, triggers the auditory hallucination of the heartbeat.
observable_conditions: Police officers sit and chat casually while the narrator becomes increasingly agitated (P0016)
The narrator starts to hear a ringing in his ears that persists and gains definiteness (P0016)
The sound is identified as the same heartbeat-like noise from the murder night (P0017)

relation_description: The distinctive description of the heartbeat before the murder is precisely echoed later, reinforcing the narrator's psychological breakdown and linking the real event to his guilt-induced hallucination.
stage1_rationale: The exact repetition of the distinctive simile across paragraphs creates a strong textual echo, marking a clear foreshadow-payoff relationship with a non-trivial interval.

foreshadow_text: It is impossible to say how first the idea entered my brain; but once conceived, it haunted me day and night. Object there was none. Passion there was none. I loved the old man. He had never wronged me. He had never given me insult. For his gold I had no desire. I think it was his eye! yes, it was this! He had the eye of a vulture—a pale blue eye, with a film over it. Whenever it fell upon me, my blood ran cold; and so by degrees—very gradually—I made up my mind to take the life of the old man, and thus rid myself of the eye forever.
payoff_text: But even yet I refrained and kept still. I scarcely breathed. I held the lantern motionless. I tried how steadily I could maintain the ray upon the eye. Meantime the hellish tattoo of the heart increased. It grew quicker and quicker, and louder and louder every instant. The old man’s terror must have been extreme! It grew louder, I say, louder every moment!—do you mark me well? I have told you that I am nervous: so I am. And now at the dead hour of the night, amid the dreadful silence of that old house, so strange a noise as this excited me to uncontrollable terror. Yet, for some minutes longer I refrained and stood still. But the beating grew louder, louder! I thought the heart must burst. And now a new anxiety seized me—the sound would be heard by a neighbour! The old man’s hour had come! With a loud yell, I threw open the lantern and leaped into the room. He shrieked once—once only. In an instant I dragged him to the floor, and pulled the heavy bed over him. I then smiled gaily, to find the deed so far done. But, for many minutes, the heart beat on with a muffled sound. This, however, did not vex me; it would not be heard through the wall. At length it ceased. The old man was dead. I removed the bed and examined the corpse. Yes, he was stone, stone dead. I placed my hand upon the heart and held it there many minutes. There was no pulsation. He was stone dead. His eye would trouble me no more.
foreshadow_summary: The narrator reveals that his sole motive for murder is the old man's 'vulture eye'—a pale blue eye with a film over it—which makes his blood run cold and which he wishes to rid himself of forever.
payoff_summary: After killing the old man by dragging him to the floor and pulling the bed over him, the narrator examines the corpse, confirms the heart has stopped, and states with satisfaction, 'His eye would trouble me no more.'

proposed_trigger.description: On the eighth night, when the narrator opens the lantern a tiny bit, the thin ray of light falls directly on the vulture eye, which is wide open. This sight enrages the narrator and directly triggers the murder.
observable_conditions: The narrator opens the lantern just enough to let out a single dim ray (P0008)
The ray falls upon the vulture eye, which is open (P0009)
The narrator grows furious at the sight and leaps into the room to kill the old man (P0011)

relation_description: The eye, introduced early as the primary motivation, becomes the direct trigger for the murder when it is seen open, and its threat is permanently eliminated through the killing.
stage1_rationale: The eye as a motive and its resolution are clearly linked, but the payoff is relatively direct and less delayed compared to other candidates. Nonetheless, it forms a recognizable setup and payoff structure with a moderate interval.
